what:
OpenFn adaptor for M-Pesa. The adaptor lets OpenFn workflows read from and write to M-Pesa Daraja API.

It exposes 6 operations that you can call from a workflow step, listed in the Operations tab. Vendor homepage: https://developer.safaricom.co.ke.
connect:
Safaricom supports OAuth 2.0 or HTTP Basic auth for API access.

The credential schema asks for baseUrl, short_code (required), pass_key (required), consumer_key (required), and consumer_secret (required). Point the credential at the base URL that matches your environment: https://api.safaricom.co.ke, https://sandbox.safaricom.co.ke. Auth reference: https://developer.safaricom.co.ke/Documentation.
when:
Reach for this adaptor when your workflow needs to read or write data in M-Pesa Daraja API.

Triggers haven't been catalogued yet — drive the workflow from a cron schedule or an upstream webhook in the meantime. Safaricom can push events to OpenFn via webhooks (docs: https://developer.safaricom.co.ke/Documentation), so reactive workflows are a good fit.
wire:
A typical wiring pattern is trigger → fetch → transform → write.

Drive the workflow from a cron schedule or an upstream webhook trigger. Then chain operations from this adaptor's Operations tab to fetch or write data in Safaricom. Pass values between steps through state.data — every operation reads from and writes to job state.

To move data between systems, follow this step with another adaptor (e.g. write the fetched records into a downstream FHIR, DHIS2, or database adaptor).
scale:
When running Safaricom jobs at volume:

- Prefer Safaricom webhooks over polling — they cut latency and rate-limit pressure.
- Paginate list and search calls explicitly; never assume a single response is the full result set.
- Make jobs idempotent by keying on stable external IDs so a replay doesn't create duplicates.
- Surface 4xx/5xx responses with the vendor request ID in your logs, and treat 429s as retries with backoff.

Adapter note: Kenya M-Pesa via Safaricom Daraja. Consumer key/secret obtain OAuth bearer; passkey used for STK Push password hashing.
best:
- Store Safaricom credentials in OpenFn's credential store; never inline keys or tokens in job code.
- Pin @openfn/language-mpesa to v1.1.13 in package.json so an upstream change doesn't break a production workflow silently.
- Keep each step small — one logical API call per operation makes failures easier to diagnose and retry.
- Add defensive checks before transforming inbound payloads — vendor responses change between versions.
- Log enough context (resource id, run id, vendor request id) so an incident can be traced end-to-end.
